events.title: A game apparatus is patented before a game industry exists
events.summary: Goldsmith and Mann's cathode-ray tube amusement patent shows electronic display play before the later commercial game industry.
events.detail: It belongs in the prehistory because it separates electronic play from stored-program computer games and raster-TV consumer products.

events.title: OXO makes a computer answer back
events.summary: A. S. Douglas's OXO is an early graphical computer game on EDSAC.
events.detail: It is a real and important game, but a different category from domestic raster-television apparatus.

events.title: Tennis for Two turns a laboratory display into public play
events.summary: William Higinbotham's analogue tennis exhibit made a scientific display intentionally playful for visitors.
events.detail: Its oscilloscope trace and open-day context matter precisely because they differ from later raster TV and consumer products.

events.title: Spacewar! creates a culture of shared code
events.summary: MIT's Spacewar! joined real-time control, simulation and competitive play on the PDP-1.
events.detail: It is foundational for programmer culture even though the minicomputer context was not a mass home or arcade market.

events.title: Baer, Rusch and Harrison make the television negotiable
events.summary: The Brown Box and Magnavox Odyssey move play toward the ordinary raster-scan television and a manufactured home platform.
events.detail: The pioneer-patent distinction is not that earlier systems were not games; it is that the claimed apparatus used player-controlled symbols, hitting, coincidence and response on television-type raster display.

events.title: Spacewar! ideas become a coin-operated cabinet
events.summary: Nolan Bushnell and Ted Dabney's Computer Space for Nutting Associates tested whether minicomputer play could be commercialised with dedicated circuitry.
events.detail: It was commercially derived from the ideas demonstrated by Spacewar!, but the cabinet was Computer Space, not Spacewar! itself.

events.title: Pong makes television play immediately legible
events.summary: Pong demonstrated that a simple video game could work commercially in public venues.
events.detail: The home-console and arcade routes now had visible business forms: product manufacture and pay-per-play.

events.title: The ZX80 breaks a household price threshold
events.summary: Sinclair's ZX80 made a programmable home computer thinkable for many households.
events.detail: Clive Sinclair remains central because price and availability changed who could imagine becoming a programmer.

events.title: ZX81 and BBC Micro create two routes into computing
events.summary: Sinclair's affordability route and the BBC/Acorn educational route worked differently but both widened access.
events.detail: The North East account should test how schools, local education authorities, clubs and Acorn-market publishers shaped access rather than assuming a single cause.

events.title: The ZX Spectrum boots into a programming culture
events.summary: The Spectrum normalised a machine that was both game player and programming environment.
events.detail: BASIC was resident in ROM, and PEEK, POKE, USR, DATA, CLEAR, SAVE CODE, loaders and assemblers opened a path toward machine code.

events.title: Code travels on paper
events.summary: Magazines, books, newsletters, clubs, libraries, cassette swapping and friends carried executable ideas through print and tape.
events.detail: A listing was software distribution, programming lesson, installation, debugging exercise, modifiable source code, route into game design and bridge from player to developer.

events.title: The Acorn Electron widens the Acorn route
events.summary: The Electron offered a lower-cost Acorn machine and appears repeatedly in surviving North East author accounts.
events.detail: This does not prove a single regional cause; it sets up the research question about schools, publishers and local networks.

events.title: Tynesoft begins in Blaydon
events.summary: Tynesoft becomes the anchor case for how a regional software house turned code into products.
events.detail: The site should describe education software, games and publishing, cassette duplication, artwork, retail, licences, reviews and multiple incompatible 8-bit computers while keeping legal details open until verified.

events.title: Gary Partis moves from BASIC into 6502 machine code
events.summary: A&B Computing profile records an early path through PET BASIC, BBC Micro ownership, 6502 machine code and early commercial work.
events.detail: This is useful testimony for the technical path from school/home access to professional programming.

events.title: A Washington computer-club recollection shows where programmers may have met
events.summary: Stardot testimony recalls a computer club at The Galleries in Washington and later Oxclose Community Centre, with Kevin Blake and Jason Sobell mentioned.
events.detail: Use as testimony, not as unquestionable fact; it belongs in the public research-status section as a lead for documentary corroboration.

events.title: Icon and Audiogenic/ASL reveal a freelance publishing network
events.summary: Kevin Blake and Gary Partis material links Icon, Audiogenic/ASL and individual authors, but the exact organisational relationship is not settled.
events.detail: The implementation must distinguish an organisational successor from staff, rights, commissioned work or individual author movement.

events.title: Micropower publishes Doctor Who and the Mines of Terror
events.summary: BBC Micro archive records Gary Partis as author and Micro Power as publisher.
events.detail: It illustrates an external publisher with a North East author, not a North East company.

events.title: Tynesoft scales through sports, licences and conversions
events.summary: Kevin Blake's portfolio makes visible the labour behind Tynesoft's sports and licensed conversion work.
events.detail: Program -> working game -> cassette master -> duplication -> packaging and artwork -> instructions -> advertising -> reviews -> mail order or retail -> royalties and accounting -> support -> ports and conversions -> licensed properties -> wages, deadlines and financial risk.

events.title: Reflections enters the Acorn market through Superior
events.summary: Ravenskull and Codename: DROID are used as early Reflections leads, with Superior as external publisher.
events.detail: Driver and Ubisoft should remain a short bridge; the Phase 0 point is early technical work, publishing relationship and transition toward a durable company.

events.title: Zeppelin appears as a post-Tynesoft staff-heritage lead
events.summary: The supplied poster connects Brian Jobling and Zeppelin to Tynesoft heritage; Companies House supports the later name-chain but not every origin claim.
events.detail: This must be shown as staff/founder heritage unless official records prove legal continuity.

events.title: Tynesoft closes as the 8-bit business model reaches its limit
events.summary: First-person recollections describe closure and post-closure movement into other work; official insolvency records should still be added.
events.detail: The public site should omit allegations and focus on market transition, staff knowledge and the difference between company closure and skills continuing.

events.title: Flair shows why staff heritage is not legal succession
events.summary: Kevin Blake recalls post-Tynesoft work involving Flair, but that is not enough to display a solid company-continuity line.
events.detail: The lineage view should show this as dashed staff/founder heritage.

events.title: The Teesside orbit develops separately from Newcastle/Tyneside
events.summary: Optimus, Iguana UK and Acclaim Studios Teesside should be researched as a parallel regional line rather than folded into Newcastle's story.
events.detail: The public map should keep Stockton, Middlesbrough and Teesside visible as a separate orbit.

events.title: Pitbull extends the later regional racing-game line
events.summary: Pitbull Syndicate belongs in the lineages-forward epilogue and Teesside/Tyne-Wear orbit.
events.detail: Use to show later staff movement and acquisitions without overwhelming early formation.

events.title: North East games sector is marketed as a modern cluster
events.summary: Invest Newcastle highlights Atomhawk, Ubisoft Reflections and People Can Fly Gateshead as modern sector examples.
events.detail: The epilogue should connect this modern cluster to skills, education and company movement, not imply a single unbroken lineage from the 1980s.

events.title: The Game Technology Lab formalises game engineering in Newcastle
events.summary: Newcastle University and supplied case-study material connect Graham Morgan, Game Lab, games teaching, industry ties and regional motivation.
events.detail: Phase 0 should end by showing how technical, educational and industry networks make a later university lab plausible.
